title: Northeast Fisheries Heritage Protection Act of 2025
short_title: Northeast Fisheries Heritage Protection Act of 2025
plain_summary: This bill would stop companies from building large wind turbines in the ocean in a specific area of the Gulf of Maine where lobster fishing happens. The goal is to protect the fishing jobs and businesses in New England that depend on catching fish and lobsters in those waters. The bill also requires the government to do a study within four months to make sure they are properly checking how offshore wind projects might hurt the environment in the Gulf of Maine. The ban on wind projects in this fishing area would be permanent and could never be changed.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Northeast Fisheries Heritage Protection Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Prohibition on Commercial Offshore Wind Energy Development in Lobster Management Area 1
sections.text: Congress has found these facts. The New England fishing industry has been an important part of the area's history, culture, and economy for over four hundred years. In two thousand twenty three, New England commercial fisheries caught about four hundred sixteen million pounds of fish worth over one billion three hundred fifty million dollars. This supports thousands of fishermen, their families, and communities that depend on ocean resources. The United States lobster fishery is one of the most valuable fisheries in the country. Maine and Massachusetts produce ninety three percent of the lobster harvest. Commercial lobster landings totaled one hundred thirteen million pounds worth five hundred eighty two million dollars. Lobster Management Area 1 in the Gulf of Maine is a very important and productive fishing area. Fishermen catch halibut, tuna, flounder, redfish, black sea bass, monkfish, mackerel, rock and sand crab, mussels, hardshell and softshell clams, oysters, scallops, conchs, squid, and lobster there. In two thousand twenty four, the Biden Administration's Bureau of Ocean Energy Management announced the Final Wind Energy Area for commercial offshore wind development in the Gulf of Maine. This area did not include any part of Lobster Management Area 1. President Trump recently issued an Executive Order that temporarily stopped wind energy leasing in all areas of the outer Continental Shelf. He said this was important for marine life and to make sure the United States can maintain a strong fishing industry. Permanently protecting Lobster Management Area 1 from commercial energy leasing and development would avoid conflict with New England commercial and recreational fishing industries. It would also preserve an important fishing area for future generations. Even though section eight of the Outer Continental Shelf Lands Act allows it, no lease, license, permit, or other permission can be given for developing commercial offshore wind energy in Lobster Management Area 1. Within one hundred twenty days after this law is passed, the Comptroller General must do a study. The study will look at whether the environmental review processes used by the National Marine Fisheries Service, the Bureau of Ocean Energy Management, and other relevant federal agencies are good enough for offshore wind projects in the Gulf of Maine. The study must look at how offshore wind projects in the Gulf of Maine affect marine mammals including whales, finfish, benthic resources (animals and plants that live on or near the ocean floor), the commercial and recreational fishing industries including marine equipment retailers, onshore processors, fish markets, and other shoreside businesses, air quality and greenhouse gas emissions, cultural historical and Tribal resources, marine invertebrates (ocean animals without backbones), essential fish habitat (areas needed by fish to spawn breed feed or grow to maturity as defined by federal law), plankton abundance and distribution, recreation and tourism, and fisheries dependent communities. The study must also look at how each agency decides which stakeholders (people with an interest in the outcome) to talk to about offshore wind projects in the Gulf of Maine. It must check if local representatives and interested parties get enough time to comment on these wind projects. In this section, Lobster Management Area 1 means the same thing as EEZ Nearshore Management Area 1 as defined in section six hundred ninety seven point eighteen of title fifty of the Code of Federal Regulations.
